desc:
A small cottage and a number of smaller outbuildings once stood here. But all of
the structures have been torn to the ground. Rough piles of stone show where the
walls once rose, but of the contents of the buildings nothing now remains. Some
charring on the stones suggests that the entire holding was fired, and there is
nothing here to tell the tale of how or when this disaster fell.
name: At a Ruined Smallholding

desc:
Clearly this land was once cared for and tended, for an extremely tall and broad
dry stone wall protects it to both the south and east. The wall is one of quite
heroic proportions for a simple farm, being built twice as thick as one would
expect. And its height too is impressive. But apparently this was not enough to
protect the owners of these fields. Judging by the weeds and rough shrubs which
have overrun the area, something forced the farmers to leave the area some time
ago.
name: Forgotten Farmland

desc:
An extremely tall and stout dry stone wall has been constructed to the north of
here. Although it shows signs of a prolonged period of disrepair, with no small
number of stones having toppled from place, it remains in very sound shape,
making it impossible to breach. But why it was built seems a mystery, for the
land is here is nothing but barren, blustery plains, covered with nothing more
threatening than coarse grasses, and small tufts of heather.
name: South of a Stout Wall

desc:
The thick forest which rises to the north and east is steadily spreading out
over the land. Already this area is partially covered by dense brambles and
cruelly spiked briers. Tall, but strangely twisted saplings reach up through the
choking grasp of the undergrowth. Coarse, yellow weeds and sickly, thin grasses
grow everywhere there is any space between the tangled bushes.
name: In the Encroaching Undergrowth

desc:
Though offered a little protection from the elements by a forest to the north,
the land here remains blustery and windswept. Coarse, low-growing grasses and
thick tufts of heather thrive in this environment, but only a few quite stunted
bushes and shrubs rise above the ground. Closer to the forest, the vegetation
seems to do better, rising to greater heights, but further south and west, the
aspect becomes ever more bleak.
name: On a Wild and Open Plain

desc:
Ancient and hoary trees crowd one upon another in this area of woodland. Part of
a larger forest, which stretches out before you, both northeast and south- east
of here, this coppice has a decidedly unwholesome air. Perhaps this is why the
inhabitants of the smallholding which used to stand to the west took the time to
construct such a tall and stout wall at its borders. The trees are all quite
stunted, although large in girth, and their trunks seem to twist and writhe as
they climb into the air. The branches tangle together above you, to make a thick
canopy through which no natural light can pass.
name: In a Dark and Twisted Copse

desc:
A path of trees, leading in from the north, comes to a sudden stop here, as if
pointing to a lonely, broken tree. Surrounded by a small circle of bushes, the
solitary giant stands just to the east of here, and overhead the canopy is
higher and opens slightly around the solemn tree.
name: Shadowed Forest

desc:
Tall trees loom above you now and seem to grow in a narrow line from north to
south. The low branches cloud the top of the trees, and no outside noises can be
heard from this part of the forest. Looking to the east and west, nothing can be
seen beyond the trees that immediately surround you. Far to the south the forest
is encompassed in darkness.
name: Shadowed Forest

desc:
A strange darkness fills the forest in every direction. The branches above seem
to bend very low to the forest floor, and many vines crawl their way up to the
canopy of the forest. A line of trees can be seen coming from the south leading
to an unknown destination.
name: Shadowed Forest

desc:
The bank of the water opens to a small landing beside a forest in the north.
Tree roots and rocks can be seen in the shallow section of the stream here. The
streambed turns its course from east to south here.
name: Winding Stream

desc:
Boulders climb up from underneath the surface making travel difficult. Tree
roots can also be seen nourishing in the shallows of the stream. The banks of
the water wind their way from north to west along the forest.
name: Winding Stream

desc:
The water is wider here than at any other point. The bottom of the water can no
longer be seen except along the very edge that leads to a high shoulder on the
eastern and western shores. The stream continues to flow from north to south
here, straightening slightly from its twisting passage through the lands to the
north.
name: Winding Stream

desc:
Water begins to shallow here, making it easy to cross. The lands to the east are
completely open, a contrast to the forest of the west. A strange tree dominates
your view of the forest to the west, old and full of large knots.
name: Winding Stream

desc:
The land seems to once have been rich and full of life, yet lack of travel and
concern has led to many tall bushes that grow out of control. Weeds dominate the
ground. The ground is very soggy as though the stream overflowed here recently,
filling the plain.
name: Overgrown Shoulder

desc:
The mud at the side of the river is deep and wide, forcing travellers to pass
through it to reach the waters. Slowly to the east the ground becomes more firm
and a lush countryside appears, whereas the land in the north seems untamed by
comparison.
name: Brown Shoulder

desc:
The muddy shoulder of the river to the west becomes more firm, and sprouts grass
a foot high. Groups of bushes and vegetation are scattered across the field. A
large forest towers above the western lands, a harsh comparison to the seemingly
endless land to the east.
name: High Plains

desc:
Grasses of many different yellow and green hues grow in abundance all around the
floor of the plain. Most of the grass is short, with no one tuft standing any
taller than its neighbours. A growth of wild weeds dominates the western land,
where a winding waterway can be seen beyond a low wall of grass.
name: High Plains

desc:
Grass of about half of a foot high dominates the land around you. The ground
soft and moist, and though a wall of high grass and thin reeds blocks your view,
the sound of running water betrays the presence of a nearby stream. The prairie
opens to the south and east here.
name: Prairie

desc:
Tall bushels of brown and green grass flow in the breeze all around. A hint of a
trail is here, trampled long ago by feet, leading to the east and west. The
grass of the plains grow high here and to the west, whereas the land opens more
to the east where a lush, colourful field can be seen. A thick clump of bushes
blocks passage northward.
name: High Plains

desc:
Slowly the field sinks down and the ground becomes soft and moist. Tall, thorny
bushes grow in a circle around the swampland with many small insects swarming
any animal with blood that dares enter. The bushes become thick to the south,
making the fields to the north, east and west the more desirable ways to travel.
name: Slight Depression

desc:
The ground beneath you is rich in a variety of brown and green hues. The foliage
is diverse and grows in abundance in the absence of any large groups of trees.
The land is open in all directions, as if only awaiting exploration.
name: An Open Field

desc:
A mile of flat, pebbly ground, dotted in shrubbery and weed, leads to the ford
in the river Bruinen, where the foamy waters open their way noisily through the
rocks. A path of compact earth cuts through the plain, showing obvious evidence
of heavy use, yet bearing a surprisingly well-kept appearance. At the edge of
the levelled riverside, it turns northwards and winds uphill. On the west, a
dense forest carpets the southern face of that hillock with deep-green shades.
name: West of the Ford in the Bruinen

desc:
A few flat, smooth-edged, slippery-looking stones stick out of the shallow, yet
troubled waters in this fordable section of the river. The stream dashes
fiercely and noisily against them, like a tireless battering ram, but the rocks
tear it to shreds of white, splashing foam. This is the only ford for many miles
round about, and here is where the Great East Road crosses the Bruinen.
name: A Ford in the River Bruinen

desc:
The field opens to rows of trees and plants. Once, someone had taken great care
in planting, now weeds sprout up among the plants and few leaves grow on the
trees. Molehills show that the soil a few inches deep is rich and able to
sustain life, but the top layer is loose and dry.
name: Cultivated Fields

desc:
Horse tracks dominate the ground beneath you. Grass and hay lie in abundance
throughout the meadow, a perfect grazing land for horses. The ground is soft and
many plants grow tall and colourful in the fertile ground. The meadow continues
to the lands in the south and east.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
Piles of hay gathered in huge cubes fill the meadow for the grazing animals that
pass through. The grass consists of many colours, shapes, and sizes, from tall,
thin dark green needlelike grass to yellow leaf-like blades. The meadow
stretches out before you to the east, west and south, but to the north the Old
East Road cuts a scar across the land.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
White and grey stones litter the ground below you in no partictular order or
pattern. Grass and weeds grow from among the stones, but the grass is short and
even, unlike the meadow far to the west. A road traverses the countryside to the
north along the southern border of a tall forest.
name: Stony Meadow

desc:
Short green and yellow grass grows here and contrasts the beautiful variety of
plants and flowers that grow in this part of the meadow. The grass dies quickly
to the north as the road passes through, but grows richly to the south and west.
name: Meadow beside the Road

desc:
Close by the roadway, the grass grows short and looks rather yellow. But
wildflowers proliferate nonetheless, littering the entire landscape with their
bright colours, and stretching into the distance, to the east, west and south.
Both flowers and grass seem to crowd around the trunks of the few scattered
pines, as if seeking the shade of their dark-green tops.
name: Meadow Dotted in Pine Trees

desc:
A few sturdy pine trees grow right up to the roadside here, their branches
reaching out over the thoroughfare, as if willing to offer shelter to weary
travellers passing by. Thick grass and tall bushes carpet the flat ground.
However, a few of them creep beyond the trees and onto the rocky surface, as
though jealous of the territory borrowed by the road, threatening to encroach
upon it.
name: Dispersed Pines along the Road

desc:
Though the hill top does not stand all that high above the surrounding lands, it
is nevertheless extremely exposed, and virtually no vegetation manages to cling
to its stony crown. To the east and south a forest wraps around the foot of the
hill. To the west lies only a vast open plain. In contrast, the northern face
plummets down into a deep cutting, through which the Great East Road runs.
name: A Wind-beaten Hill

desc:
Cedars abound in this raised area of the forest, as though they had migrated
here over the years, perhaps trying to be as close as possible to the sun. On
the north, the ground comes to a sudden end in the escarp face of a gully. But
the trees crowd up to the very edge of the wall, and even further, as some of
them hang precariously from the cliff top. To the east and south, the terrain
descends to the encounter of the near Bruinen river.
name: Elevated Woodland

desc:
Although the trees never lose all of their leaves, they renew them slowly every
year, and the fallen ones gather in a thick layer, carpeting the ground like a
colourful mantle. Dark green and dun shades abound the most, but also yellowish,
ochre, and brown hues. It's not uncommon to hear the creak of the dead leaves
beneath the feet of some small beast. And it mixes with the merry singing of the
birds, especially in the sunny summer afternoons.
name: An Evergreen Woodland

desc:
The soil must be extraordinarily fertile in this area, bathed by the clean,
foamy waters of the Bruinen. The trees grow closely, but that doesn't seem to
take any bit of their vitality away. Hundreds of pinecones, large and small, lie
half-buried among the fallen leaves. The unmistakable sound of flowing water
reaches your ears from the southeast, but there the wood comes to a sudden end
in a high embankment that towers over the unapproachable stream.
name: Lush Forest Overlooking the River

desc:
You are now immersed in a gentle-looking, lively forest. On sunny days,a
glimmering cascade of light filters in through the canopy. When the nights are
clear, the bright starlight dyes the trees in silvery shades. Both the youngest
saplings and the millennial, lofty trunks bear a wholesome look upon them, the
tender buds dotting their branches with every new spring.
name: Evergreen Forest

desc:
This section of the grasslands is relatively protected from the harsh winds that
blow from the mountains. It lies somewhat squeezed between the leafy forest that
stands on the east and the sheer wall that ascends to a higher plateau
southwards. Vegetation grows wild and flowers abound, perhaps swept from the
stretching plains to the northwest, and dropped here.
name: Sheltered Lowlands

desc:
Although this is certainly an uneven region, dotted with prominent ridges, sharp
bluffs, deep cuttings and rounded hillocks, this particular area seems to have
been embedded into the landscape, for its lack of features and its dreary
flatness. Somehow, the whole plain appears to have sunken as, a few miles
further, the terrain ascends to the south and east.
name: On an Open Plain

desc:
Around you is a depression filled with many varieties of coloured grasses. Tall,
brown stalks to the east give way to a plain of green grass that eventually
turns to yellow needlelike leaves to the west. A wide clearing rises above you
to the north, and a strange bed of dark green grass covers the western part of
the depression.
name: A Deep Depression

desc:
Tall brown and yellow stalks of grass sway in the shifting breeze all around the
meadow. The ground is firm and dry and few other plants grow in the meadow. A
bed of green grass seems out of place in the eastern part of the meadow.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
The grass here is a consistent shade of green, dominating the horizon to the
north and east. A small patch of grass grows strangely taller on the western
part of the meadow. It is of a different colour than the rest of the meadow.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
The fields give way to a small circular clearing. Boulders lie in a circle
around the remains of many campfires. The ground here seems strangely soft for
being so far away from the stream. A field lies to the west here and a strange
patch of ground can be seen to the east of the camp.
name: A Small Clearing

desc:
Somehow the land here is touched by neither weeds nor tracks. Recently planted
shrubs grow along the southern end of the field and enclose a rich diversity of
other plants and trees. A plain lies to the west and the vegetation opens
slightly to the east.
name: Heavily Cultivated Fields

desc:
Rising a little the land here opens to rows of weed dominated plants. Very few
plants remain untouched by the weeds, those that survive only do so barely,
growing low to the ground. The fields continue in all directions, though in the
west a low valley can be seen in the distance.
name: Cultivated Fields

desc:
The land here blends into the surrounding land making it hard to judge the
distance travelled. A variety of green grass grows in abundance here, a perfect
grazing field for horses, ponies, and cows. The meadow grows rich in the lands
to the far east and north.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
Tall stalks of grass shift in the breeze around you like the whitecaps of a
lake. The ground is soft and wet but this part of the meadow is less cultivated
than the surrounding meadows in the west and south.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
Plants, grass, and weeds of a large variety grow in this part of the meadow. The
colourful array of shapes and sizes draw your eyes away from the lands around
you. The meadow continues to grow in the lands to the west and south.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
The ground becomes firm and brown, the grass parts, and a circular clearing
forms. The ground is littered with remains of previous camps, and animal dens.
Stones formed in circles for campfires litter the ground beneath you. The land
is green and fertile to the north, and slopes down to the south.
name: A Wide Clearing

desc:
A grass-carpeted prairie extends all over this area. Wild flowers dot the ground
here and there, adding a touch of colour to the green background. The wind howls
throughout the open plain, the tall grass waving to and fro as in time with it.
Standing in the middle of this wide expanse, everything would probably look
smaller and less important, in comparison to the apparently boundless
grasslands.
name: Rolling Prairie

desc:
A bunch of small, leafy, lively-looking pine trees grow together here, as if
expelled from the wholesome woodland that stands only a couple of miles further
to the east. All about the trees, countless needle-like leaves lie scattered
among the lush grass, carried along by the wind all over the nearby plains. All
in all, the tiny forest looks quite curious, the mixed undergrowth showing many
different green shades.
name: A Small Clump of Pines in the Grasslands

desc:
Tall evergreens thrive here on the lower reaches of a small hillside. But unlike
the more famous and feared forests of this part of the world, this one stands
open and inviting, its lofty canopy being thin enough to permit light and
refreshing breezes to penetrate all the way to the forest floor. But it is
during the sunny spring days that the forest blooms in all its splendour, when
the leaves shine in their brightest green hues.
name: Green, Wooded Hillside

desc:
The river continues to wind through these uneven dales. Here the stream bends
from east to south, in the shadow of a tall embankment of worn rock, crowned by
a dense forest. Some of the trees seem to stretch their limbs out towards the
river, as if willing, though incapable, to touch the surface. It is not uncommon
to hear the deep sound of a little branch dropping down into the waters, nor to
find a pinecone or needle floating on them.
name: Bend in the River Bruinen

desc:
Slowly but steadily, the Bruinen - that the men call Loudwater - continues its
sinuous course through these rugged lands. Many leagues downstream, to the
southwest, it meets with another great river that flows down from the frozen
northern vales. The elven people call that river Mitheithel, but its name is
Hoarwell in the tongue of the men. And from there on it is known as Gwathlo, or
Greyflood, and it cuts through all of Eriador until it meets with the Great Sea
at Lond Daen.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
Even here, a mile downstream from the ford, there is still a lot of turbulence
in the water. Strong underwater currents zigzag through the rock-strewn bottom,
forming dangerous-looking whirlpools in the surface. But little by little the
streambed deepens, and the flow slows down. Here the river takes a sharp turn to
the west, avoiding the upright walls of a protruding ridge.
name: Troubled Waters South of a Ford

desc:
A stony trail climbs this steep incline, the first one off the stream in these
folded lands. The hillside shows the dun colour of matted earth, sprinkled with
pebbles and boulders here and there, as if carried along and then left by a
flood of the river. Perhaps for that same reason, only a few thorny brambles
grow here, their yellowish hues half-camouflaged by the ground.
name: A Path along a Steep, Brown Bank

desc:
Like a thin, blurry shadow, at times strengthened, at other times diminishing,
the path imitates the bends of the river. But with every step, it seems to draw
slightly closer to the stream, as both go away snaking to the southwest. A
protruding ridge stands directly to the west, forcing the path to take a sharp
turn from east to south here. Beyond that bend, it disappears into the
grasslands.
name: Trail Approaching the River

desc:
A small, flat-topped pinnacle of rock stands in the path of the Bruinen river,
forcing the stream to detour it. The waters bathe the northern and western
faces, that look heavily broken and dangerously slippery. The other hillsides,
on the contrary, descend gently into the uneven plains that stretch out in the
distance, blending with them. A trail can be glimpsed cutting through the fields
down there. Overlooking the river, a deep-green wood spreads to the very edge of
a high embankment above the flow.
name: A Ridge Overlooking the River

desc:
Large, wet boulders covered in thick moss seem to sprout out of the riverside,
forming a stony fence that blocks any attempt to reach the stream. The ground
goes sharply lower to the edge of that cliff-like rockface above the waters.
Here and there a lonely oak stands atop the rocky wall, stretching its limbs out
over the grey barrier, like long fingers willing to cool off in the river.
name: A Rock-bristled Riverside

desc:
Running in-between the thick thorns, the trail is hard to follow, disappearing
almost completely from time to time. It is almost as if there was a wall of
living bushes, moving around, trying to make the passage as hard as possible.
The trail itself is old and obviously used rarely.
name: An Old Trail

desc:
The trail has become rather obscured and seemingly hidden from view, as the
thorns tower over it on both sides. Strange echoes and weird glimpses of
movement can be seen from within as they sway in the wind, like living walls all
around you. The thorns are immensely thick and impassable, except to the north
where a path has been cleared through them.
name: An Obscured Trail

desc:
This path has been hacked through the great thick thorns that seem eager to
swallow the trail and cover every trace of it with their branches. Not much can
be seen or heard, except the rustle of the thorns in the wind and an occasional
bird. It should be possible to enter the dense thorns to the west, but to the
east some bushes obstruct the way.
name: A Path through the Bushes

desc:
Just south of the old stone bridge the path cuts through a small area of dense
thornbushes, which tower over the trail and block any view of the surroundings.
Judging by the dead branches lying on the trailside the thorns are pruned
regularly, keeping the trail passable. The only other thing that catches the eye
is a stone tower rising from a ruined keep, some distance to the southeast.
name: Path in the Bushes

desc:
This old stone bridge stretches across a yawning crevice, from which the
rumbling sound of fast flowing water can be heard. Remarkably well preserved,
there is not a single sign that the elements have marred it in any way. The
stone is still as smooth and finely chiselled as the day the bridge was built,
with several different carvings still visible on the railings. The trail
continues on both sides of the bridge, disappearing into some huge thorny bushes
to the south. Further to the southeast a tower can be seen, but at this distance
no details can be discerned.
name: An Old Stone Bridge

desc:
This old trail used to be well trodden, but nowadays it is abandoned and empty,
used only by villains and those who want to stay out of sight. The trail itself
is covered by gravel, the remains of the smooth stone that paved it in ancient
days. Surrounded by plains trying to recover the area stolen from it, the trail
fights an unfair and losing battle against nature.
name: An Old Trail

desc:
This section of the road seems to be a transition from a larger trail to the
south into a more defined road, somewhat elevated from the surrounding plains.
It runs in a roughly north-south line, dividing a large plain into a bushier
section to the west, and a greener section to the east that looks as if it may
have once been cultivated.
name: Ancient Road

desc:
The road bends east and south here, curving past a meadow located just to the
north. Although the area does not seem to be farmed, the soil and climate both
seem hospitable. Animal tracks can be seen leading north towards the green
grass, as well as west towards the bushes. The plains seem to continue on
northward for miles.
name: Bend in the Ancient Road

desc:
The road runs north and west, curving beyond a large grassy area to the east and
forming the northern edge of a large plain to the south of here. The surface is
hard-packed dirt with a few scattered rocks on the ground here and there. The
edges of the road are lined with rocks that may have once been placed there
deliberately, but now have a haphazard appearance, having been knocked about
over the years by patrols and other large groups moving through the area.
name: A Bend in the Road

desc:
The road runs a straight north-south course here, diving between two grassy
fields that could be plots of farmland were they not still and quiet, except for
the occasional traveller or animals passing through. The entire region seems to
have been neglected over time, as if it were somehow unfit for habitation by
anyone.
name: Ancient Road

desc:
The road continues on a north-south path, almost as straight as an arrow. The
landscape to the west is rather barren, with only a few patches of grass growing
here and there near the road. Off in the distance in that direction, one can
barely see a large outcropping or some sort of cliff that seems to grow up out
of the surrounding plains.
name: Ancient Road

desc:
The road travels from a large junction to the north straight south for several
miles. To the east the road slopes downward into a thick, grassy field, quite a
stark contrast from the barren, rocky landscape visible to the west. The terrain
to the west offers little to the traveller, whereas the east seems to be a good
resting place for mounts, or to take a break from the road.
name: Ancient Road

desc:
This large junction of the north-south road and a narrow trail running west and
east seems to be a meeting place of some sort. Large rocks have been erected to
offer some small respite from the elements, but years of weather have worn them
down, making them all but useless as shelter. The narrow trail runs west along
the river and east into an overgrown section, seemingly the less travelled of
the two directions. The road itself continues on as a hard-packed dirt surface
moving steadily north towards the rapids, and south towards grassy plains.
name: Old Junction

desc:
The road bends here from east to south, winding along the high ground above the
River Bruinen. The River flows rapidly southward, hitting the wall below and
creating a small whirlpool from the backwash. To the west of here the river has
slowed down quite a bit as it pours into a deeper course. A small junction lies
just to the south of here.
name: Trail at a Rapid River

desc:
The road runs along a straight course here as hard, packed dirt. A few pebbles
and small rocks break up the otherwise solid reddish brown surface. Towards the
east grass seems to spring up out of nowhere, and small flowers grow alongside
the road, leading towards a full field of bright flowers. The southern section
of the road leads onto an old trail that has been overgrown with bushes.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
This section of road seems to have been formed out of a high river bluff that
has slowly eroded away over the centuries. The road runs east and south here,
following the Bruinen river as it picks up speed and depth on its southward
course. The ground slopes gently towards the river and is adorned with very few
plants or trees.
name: Trail by a Swift-flowing River

desc:
The dirt road bends north and west. A few rocks are scattered across its surface
and some larger boulders lie to the sides of the road. On the southern edge a
field of brightly flowering plants and shrubs seems to spring up out of a grassy
field, whereas the surroundings to the east appear to be just green grass with
clover cropping up intermittently.
name: Trail at Mixed Fields

desc:
The stream to the east has soaked up so much water from the lands around it,
that it has become a small river when it flows into here. A shadowy finger of
the northern forests touches the north bank of the river and provides a bit of
shade for that edge of the river.
name: Small River

desc:
The stream is slowly beginning to grow and deepen as it leaves the forested
areas north of here. Runoff from the plains to the east, and from atop the cliff
to the south, adds to the stream. With no massive forest to drink from the
stream, or to prevent water from making it to the stream, it has begun to deepen
and widen here. To the west it might even grow to be a real river. The stream
has dug into the southern slope of crumbling rock, turning it into a near
vertical wall of rock that is too unsafe to climb.
name: Large Stream

desc:
This bank, of the stream to the west, is not very firm. Water from the stream
has crept its way underground and up to near the surface here. Holes here are
quickly filled by water from the surrounding moist soil. Only in a few spots,
where stone lies close under the surface, is the soil hard packed. Elsewhere the
ground becomes more like the dragging mud that can be found in a swamp. The
vegetation here is a mixture of grasses and plants that like to grow in bogs.
Farther north the ground is moister and even less firm. Drier land lies to the
south and east where slopes rise up from here.
name: Wet Shoulder

desc:
Low green grass covers this plain with only a few shrubs to break its stark
simplicity. At the western edge of the plain is the beginning of a slope that
descends slightly and is covered in more varied vegetation than this plain. The
plain stretches out into the distance at the other compass points.
name: High Plains

desc:
A plain of rich, golden stalks of wheat is before you in any direction you turn
except to the south, where a narrow ravine cuts into the plain and begins to
slope gently downwards. No trees, shrubs, rocks, or other features break the
line of the horizon in the other directions.
name: Plains

desc:
Golden grass covers this field, intermixed with weeds and a wild mixture of
other small plants. Lines of plants show that the field was once cultivated, but
neglect and time has all but destroyed the work of the cultivators. Small thorny
shrubs block the route to the north, but nothing blocks travel to the plains in
the other directions.
name: Field of Wild Growth

desc:
This plain lies covered in small plants, perhaps placed, perhaps from seeds
blown by the wind; only hints of a pattern exist. A light smattering of small
trees covers this area, and helps to protect the soil from being blown away by
any winds that cross this flat area. Patches of grass and taller varieties of
plants grow near the western edge of this plain. A jagged outcropping of rock,
and a number of large boulders, prevent travel to the east and north.
name: Windswept Plain

desc:
A meadow of tall, green grass stretches out around you in all directions except
the west. The grass is very resistant to movement, springing back up soon after
all but the heaviest tread lifts off it. To the west a line of boulders, and a
jagged outcropping of rock, prevents travel or sight in that direction.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
Tall brown and yellow grass contests with the equally tall green grass to
dominate this meadow. A few small bushes grow around a pond that lies just
slightly south of the centre of this large meadow. The meadow stretches to the
south and west, while plains begin to the east.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
This grassy plain is a mixing area for several varieties of grass. Tall yellow
and brown grass, short bright green grass, and a more densely packed dark green
grass have grown inwards from the western, southern and eastern edges of this
plain. A deep depression lies to the north, but there is no access from this
point because of the sharp drop. The land slopes upwards gradually to the south,
and far to the south the land rises to an area that overlooks this plain.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
Sloping plains gradually rise westward towards grass-covered plains which
connect to a much higher plateau overlooking the great river Bruinen southeast
of here. The land still remembers the old road but there are few signs of it
now. A rocky wall to the south prevents passage in that direction.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
This low plateau lies to the south of some lowlands. The plains stretch for
miles to the west, sloping gently upwards and becoming less inhabited in that
direction. To the south the plains end abruptly as they encounter the near
vertical wall of a much higher plateau. The short grass underfoot is thick and
does not let even a single flower or tall weed penetrate its green body.
name: Plateau

desc:
The plateau continues on flat and fertile to the west. To the east and south the
grassy ground drops steeply into the river, winding its way through the
surrounding landscape like a jagged knife wound. Beyond the river one can make
out a lower landscape and the beginnings of some marshlands.
name: Plateau

desc:
The river cuts its way through the western plateaus, meandering through the
landscape. It flows gently towards the south here, through a river valley that
was formed many years ago. Towards the west, solid ground is too high up to
climb to without any footholds on the sheer face. The water is muddy in that
section, where it has continued to eat away at the base of the cliffs.
name: Rejuvenated Bruinen

desc:
The river flows steadily from the north, and curves gently towards the west. The
surface is calm, with small whirlpools continuously forming and breaking apart
at the southern edge. The bank there is not very steep, and it would be quite
easy to leave the water there. Along the bank are a lot of covering
river-growing weeds. The road lies just beyond, following the course of the
river.
name: Swift River Bruinen

desc:
The road runs east and south here, at the foot of a large ridge that extends
towards the north. The roar of the Bruinen River is loud, as it makes its way
southward just to the west of here. A small slope leads downward towards the
river valley, making it possible to enter the water from here. The road curves
west following the course of the river.
name: Ancient Trail

desc:
The trail widens into a large road, composed of dark brown dirt. On either side
of the roadway short grass grows tightly cropped to the ground. The river can be
heard roaring in the distance to the west. The road leads in that direction, as
well as to the north as a narrowing trail. A large field of grass grows
abundantly to the east.
name: Ancient Trail

desc:
The hard dirt surface leads north and west through grassy fields. The road is
lined with a few small shrubs and some brightly coloured flowers. The occasional
rabbit or other small animal scurries for cover at the sound of travellers and
their mounts. To the south a large pasture is visible, and would seem a likely
spot to rest on a long journey.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
The road curves east and south here, following the contours of the Bruinen River
which flows to the north and west of here. The river seems to have cut its
course out of the surrounding terrain, creating a large bluff on the far side of
it. The dark brown surface of the road is marked with hoof prints and other
signs that show it is still travelled.
name: Trail at the Bruinen

desc:
These bends in the river were formed long ago when it first cut through the
landscape, following some contours or underground rock beds along its path. The
river flows from south to west here, leaving two places where one could easily
leave the water for solid land. Roads lie to the east and south, and one could
easily assume that they meet somewhere in southeast. The water flowing west
begins to pick up speed as it heads for another bend.
name: Rejuvenated Bruinen

desc:
The river cuts its way through the plateaus, creating a deep trench in its
course east and south. High up to the north a grassy plateau is visible but it
would require climbing from here to reach it. The dirt face of the rock wall has
several roots and crevices that could serve as handholds if one wanted to scale
it. To the west however, the sheer vertical climb would be impossible. The river
continues on southward, steadily picking up speed.
name: Entrenched Meander

desc:
This is the highest of several plateaus in the surrounding area, defined by the
River Bruinen cutting its course through the landscape to the east. As it runs
south of here it begins to bend, rushing by the base of the outcropping. Far
below to the north a low-lying plain spreads out towards the east, while to the
west the ground slopes gently downwards to a grassy plain.
name: Higher Plateau

desc:
The plain climbs eastward towards a high plateau while in the northeast there is
a low plateau. There is a lot of grass on the ground but very few flowers can be
seen, making for a somewhat barren blanket of short grass. The sound of running
water indicates that a large river flows nearby. The ground to the north drops
down sharply to a lower area.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
This nearly flat plain is covered in small bushes, shrubs and a type of short,
bright green grass. A subtle slope begins near the centre of this plain and
slopes down into the east and north. The slope and its effect on water on the
plain have caused the bushes to grow more densely towards the bottom of the
slope, where more water is absorbed as it leaves here after a rainfall. The land
rises slightly to the south allowing a clear view of this plain.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
Tall green stalks of grass mix with some shrubs and numerous bushes to cover
this meadow in greenery. The plants change slowly from the dominant bushes in
the south to the more open grassy areas in the north and west. Eastwards the
land grows somewhat more barren and flat. A gentle slope leads down from here
into the south.
name: Meadow

desc:
A thick green carpet of grass dominates this large meadow. The grass blades grow
closely together and make it hard to see anything smaller than a foot tall
except for the ripples in the grass caused when something moves. The same plants
and terrain stretch outwards at the north and east sides of this meadow. Tiny
hills begin to appear near the western border of the meadow, only visible
because of the nearly perfect horizon they rise out of. A sheer cliff drops down
to the south, and beyond it in the distance is a tower on a mound that looks
down on this cliff top meadow.
name: Meadow

desc:
Deep grass covers the tiny valleys formed by small hills here, each of about two
or three feet in height. The tall grass partly hides the hills but they are
still readily apparent against the flat plains around here. The land flattens
and remains mainly covered in grass towards the north and south. The eastern and
western directions however tend to a larger variety of plants, and the land in
the east is not quite as flat as around the other edges of these hills.
name: Minor Hills

desc:
Tall green grass, intermixed with weed-choked shrubs, covers this plain. To the
west is a narrow ravine, with low cliff walls, which prevents travel that way.
The grass stretches off in all directions except the west, but in each of the
directions the shrubs make way for other types of plant life or disappear.
name: Wild Plain

desc:
This narrow ravine starts in the plains to the north and grows wider as it
deepens to the south. The low but almost vertical walls could easily be climbed
if the rock was more stable. The floor of the ravine is a gentle slope covered
in small boulders and outcroppings of rock.
name: Gently Sloping Narrow Ravine

desc:
This plain of low, green grass contrasts sharply with the higher plants and
small trees that grow in the west. The plain becomes somewhat more rocky and
less grassy towards the south. East of here is a narrow ravine, its sides are
like low cliffs, making travel impossible that way.
name: High Plains

desc:
This field is covered in foot long grass, small shrubs, and some small but
straight trees. Only a few trees exist here, and none seem to exist near the
edge of the plain. Dense, lush patches of flora grow at the northern border of
this plain, where it slopes down. Numerous small game trails go to and from here
into the watery lands in the north, and the surrounding plains.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
A flat plain of low grass, lacking trees or bushes, lies here. Dangerous slopes
drop from the western and northern edges of the field to the water below. At the
bottom of the north slope is a stream that turns there from north to west. The
western edge of this plain descends sharply to a small river.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
From the north this small river flows through here and into the west. A tall
hill with a steep slope of crumbling rock rises to the east, blocking travel, so
that travellers can only go to the south and north. Bushes in the field to the
south line the banks of the river and hang over it in many places.
name: Small River

desc:
The high plateau to the north shelters this place from the cold northern winds
during the winter. It would be difficult to climb up there from here, as the
plateau above extends over this area, making a sheltered hollow underneath it
with a depression in the dirt wall at the back. To the east a deep river flows
quickly, while a shallower section lies to the south.
name: Riverside

desc:
The river bends east and south, creating a dirt wall to the north and a deeply
cut river valley to the east. The cliff to the north has enough hand and foot
holds that one could climb up to the plateau above. The terrain to the west
seems to be an ideal area to climb out of the river, as the ground slopes gently
into the river.
name: Rapid River Bruinen

desc:
High above the river, to the east and south, is a well-travelled road. The
riverbank is steep and slippery to the east, making it hard for anyone to scale
it. The river bends abruptly to the west.
name: Entrenched Meander

desc:
The river splits here, with a section flowing eastward into an underground
waterway. Pushing through an opening low down in the eastern wall the icy black
water flows turbulently over a collection of sharp rocks and onwards through a
submerged tunnel. Westwards it becomes shallow and the already fast flowing
water gains some more speed.
name: Rapid River Bruinen

desc:
This easy swim crossing seems to never freeze, nestled between the even faster
waters to the east and the wide waters to the west. North a small sheltered area
is visible and south a narrow trail runs east and west near a high cliff.
name: Blue River Bruinen

desc:
The river flows through here from east to west, fairly wide and clear, and of
medium depth. The jagged rocks on the south bank and the northern cliff, that
overlooks the river, prevent travel in those directions, only travel upstream
and downstream is possible from here.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The river seems to be somewhat shallow here, especially on the north side where
a shallow slope begins underwater and continues above the water into the
distance northwards. The depth of the river is still too deep to attempt to ford
here without swimming or a small watercraft such as a raft. A landing can be
seen on both the south and the north shores, marking where river traffic might
be able to land.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The flow of the river here is quick despite the river being wide and deep. Clear
water here lets you see quite deep into it as it moves from the east to the
west. A giant mound of stone and earth rises to a shattered tower in the south
that overlooks the river here. The rocky riverbank makes travel to and from the
field, north of here, awkward but not impossible.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
A large river flows from the east to here, with a slightly muddy tributary
adding to the river from the south, before they flow west together. The darker
water from the tributary can easily be seen as it forces its way deep into the
heart of the Bruinen before being swept along west by the powerful current of
the river. A tall, sheer cliff blocks travel to the north.
name: Confluence of Two Rivers

desc:
The river here is slightly muddy, and is somewhat wider than upriver to the
east. There is no shore to the north where a high cliff rises above the river.
South of here is a plain that is only a bit higher than the regular flow of the
river, according to water marks on the cliff, and could easily be flooded if the
water flowing through here was even a foot higher.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The river widens a bit here as it flows into the west. A wide ravine breaks the
cliffs that wall the northern side of the river. It is possible to leave the
river to both the north and the south. The border of the field to the south is
covered in deep grass and shrubs.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The river is flowing fairly swiftly here from the east to the west under a high
cliff rising on the north side of the river. The river is narrower at the centre
of this stretch because of boulders lying along the south bank that extends into
the river. To the both the east and west the river widens somewhat. Beyond the
rocky south bank lies a rocky covered field.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The river is deep and wide at this point as it flows down this river valley. The
water flows from east to west, passing underneath a cliff that overhangs much of
the river on the north side. A field with a pebbled bank lies to the south.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
The river Bruinen rushes swiftly along its course here, from the east into the
south. A soft rushing noise comes to you from the south. A stone bank, with
watermarks up its side, prevents the river from flowing westward to the plain
there. A tall, sheer cliff borders the river to the north.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
This plain of grass and silt piles lies just above the level of the river to the
south. The plain is slightly below the river to the east with only a low stone
bank preventing this plain from being underwater all the time. Northwards the
land slopes up slightly and begins to be covered in small bushes.
name: Flood Plain

desc:
This flat, grassy bluff stands above the lands to the east, west, north and
looks down on the river to the south. A long sheer drop lies on the eastern and
southern sides, but on the western side the drop-off becomes shorter the further
to the north you go, ceasing to be a cliff at some distance to the northwest.
The view from here commands the area around it.
name: River Overlook

desc:
This gentle slope runs from the meadows in the north down to the river. It also
slopes down to the west, a bit, so that it is just above the rivers flow there,
making the highest point of the slope in the northeast, and the lowest at the
southwest. A high cliff rises to the east, preventing travel that way, and also
allowing those on top to view this slope. A few large blocks form a line from
the west to a landing here on the shore of the river.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
An abandoned quarry lies here, with blocks of stone scattered around that have
been removed from the sides of the north and west cliffs. A trail of large
blocks runs in a scattered pattern from the cliffs towards the east. Smaller,
more finely shaped blocks are piled near the cliff. All the blocks are in
excellent condition to be used in buildings, but were apparently abandoned here.
To the east lies a sloped area, and beyond it lies a cliff top which overlooks
this quarry.
name: Abandoned Quarry

desc:
The land here has been worn by wind and water, forming small gullies and ridges.
Small broad ridges of three to five feet in height run towards the north where
they have breaks in them and look like hills. Short grass covers both the ridges
and the gullies. The gullies run to the edges of cliffs east and south. To the
far east if a place on top of a cliff that can oversee these ridges.
name: Ridges

desc:
This plain is covered in foot high golden grass with a few large trees scattered
through it. Its bounded on the west by a broad ravine with high walls, and by a
cliff overlooking the river to the south. This flat land continues into the
north, but eastwards the ground begins to ripple like waves on an ocean.
name: Plain of Grass

desc:
This wide gully has a gentle slope running from the north, down to the Bruinen
in the south. Beneath the tall cliffs that form the sides of the gully are small
boulders and rocks. A smooth path has been carved into the centre of the floor
of the ravine, and it would seem that this was made by water rushing down toward
the river below. The path offers an easy route for travelling up or down the
canyon.
name: Broad Ravine

desc:
Short grass and lichen grow here, between the rocks and on them respectively. A
broad ravine cuts sharply into this plain to the east, with almost vertical
slopes on the sides. To the south is a cliff that overlooks the river and
stretches into the west.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
The sparse vegetation and thin soil here barely covers the rock of the plain.
The southern edge of this plain hangs over the river so that only part of the
river can be seen without leaning over the edge of the cliff. To the east and
west the cliff continues, and so does the sparse vegetation.
name: Plain Overhanging the River

desc:
This cliff overlooks the river to the south and is covered with short grasses,
and a few defiant trees bent from the winds that blow down the river valley. A
sheer cliff drops to the west, but to the north and east the land bordering this
plain continues to be flat.
name: Plain above the River

desc:
Low shrubs and small berry bushes cover the ground here, with small areas of
grass between them. The brush of this field grows almost over the edge of the
small river to the north. This land is bounded by a cliff to the east that seems
to grow higher as this field slopes gently down, towards the south, to a plain.
name: A Field of Small Bushes

desc:
This cave rises at a slight angle and has a small bend at the entrance. Its
shape prevents the full light of day reaching deep into the interior. The rough
natural lines of the walls have been smoothed in spots, and in other places
shelves and benches have been cut from the rock.
name: Worked Cave

desc:
The grass stands at various heights as if it were intentionally kept short near
to the road on the north and western edges. Towards the east it grows more
lushly in the damper soil. It gradually increases in height as it moves to the
south. When the air is still, one can sometimes hear the quiet roar of the
Bruinen River.
name: Flood Plain

desc:
The tall grass is very dense, and seems to spring up out of the soft ground.
Crabgrass and other weeds grow in clumps through the area, but the dominant
foliage is definitely the tall blades of grass that shift gently in the breeze,
making them come alive to the onlooker. To the south a similar scene unfolds
although without the same vigour of the grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Bright green grass grows abundantly out of the soft dirt. The southern edges of
it are marked, as if there had been a great fire. The grass there does not grow
very high, just a few inches above the ground. This green carpet continues on to
the east.
name: A Supplementary Pasture

desc:
The grass grows to a medium height with an occasional shrub or small flower
hidden among the tussocks. The earth seems to roll gently eastward, creating
small valleys. To the south the ground is much lower than on this tiny ridge,
and seems to absorb much of the runoff water from the surroundings. To the west
the earth looks barren and the vegetation blackened by fire.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Although there are no signs that the earth was recently burned, signs of a fire
long ago are everywhere. What were once trees are now blackened husks. A few are
still standing, but most are fallen logs. A bit of grass and a few weeds manage
to grow among these charred remains. Whether it was a great battle or some force
of nature is not known.
name: Burnt Field

desc:
This large field of brightly coloured flowers lies near a large bend in the road
to the northwest. In the warm season, a variety of poppies and forget-me- nots
seem to thrive among other flowers and bunches of large shrubs with bright
orange blooms. The grass underfoot is a bright green colour. To the east,
however, the field looks blackened as if it were once burned.
name: A Flower Field

desc:
You can see that here used to be a trail fording the river, since the stones
marking the passage still stand on the riverside. The trail itself is rather
overgrown and broken to the south, and disappears entirely to the north, where
it has long been submerged. The surrounding ground is rather marshy, turning
into noisome swamp to the northeast.
name: A Shallow and Marshy Part of the River

desc:
Here the river is deep and broad, flowing rapidly downstream to the west. North
lie some hills, while to the south are some old and overgrown fields that have
long been abandoned. A few birds hover over the water here trying to catch
today's meal as the river is rather rich in wildlife.
name: Broadening River

desc:
The river runs swiftly below an old stone wall here. The old lord of the castle
above seems to have used both the wall and the river for his defence, making
entering the keep from this direction near impossible. Parts of the ancient wall
have been undermined by the water and have collapsed, forcing the river to flow
along a slightly different path.
name: A River below an Ancient Stone Wall

desc:
Swift and powerful the river runs west, dragging debris and some dead trees with
it. It is hard to spot anything on the banks here at the speed the river flows
at, but one thing is noticeable - a tower above you on the cliff top. Standing
like a sentinel on the southern bank, it overlooks the surrounding land.
name: A Swift River

desc:
The river speeds up as it enters a small canyon here, making a sharp turn around
the cliffs to the west. Strange sounds echo between the walls that surround you,
as the river roars through the gorge. There seems to be no easy way to leave the
river here, as the walls are slippery and steep.
name: The Swift Tributary

desc:
Running swiftly and noisily westward, the river is deep and cold. It is almost
as if the river is eager to join with the Bruinen far downriver. The banks are
rather steep, and it would take some effort to climb up them. Not much of the
surrounding landscape beyond the river is distinctive, but one thing makes
itself known. A tower, dark and lofty, rises high enough for you to notice it on
the southern horizon.
name: A Turn in the River

desc:
Roaring westwards, deafening in its might, the river hurls itself forward in its
long search for a companion. The walls of the canyon it runs through are steep,
and impossible to climb to the south. Atop the northern bank little can be seen
except the sky, and some dense vegetation poking over the edges. The slope is
high and probably hard to climb, so leaving the river here could prove both
taxing and dangerous.
name: A Rapid River

desc:
Making a sharp turn the water plunges northwards on its journey towards the
mighty River Bruinen. Strange sounds echo between the cliff walls and white foam
flies into the air as the water smashes into the sheer rock of the walls.
name: Bend in the Tributary

desc:
Rushing swiftly westwards the white foaming water makes a deafening sound as it
echoes among the high cliff walls. A white mist hangs in the air and everything
is both wet and slippery.
name: Rapid Tributary

desc:
White mist hangs in the air as the water hurls itself over the cliff's edge. A
deafening roar drowns all other sounds and the air is damp. The steep cliff
walls surrounding this area, and everything around, is wet and slippery, thus
becoming impossible to climb. Drawing towards the edge, the water eagerly rushes
forth towards the mighty Bruinen.
name: Top of the Falls

desc:
A large waterfall, easily eighty feet tall, drops from the south to the basin
here. The water from the waterfall drops into this deep basin and then flows
westwards out of the basin as a swift river. The flow is constant and the rock
faces on the east and south cliffs are always slippery. A low, jagged wall
running from the eastern cliff blocks travel to the north.
name: Basin at the Base of the Waterfall

desc:
Swirling swiftly around the bend to the south the river bubbles and foams its
way to the north, slowing slightly as it widens and runs towards the tower
showing in the distance. To the east a narrow field sits perched precariously
between the cliff and the river, while to the west a few decayed wooden posts
appear to mark the boundary of an old pasture.
name: Swift River

desc:
This river flows swiftly from the south to here and then north in a long line.
Large ditches run from the western edge of the river deeper into the western
field. Loose earth and silt has blocked the ditches so that no water flows in
them. The eastern bank is quite rocky, but travel onto it is still possible. A
tower upon a mound lies far to the north and overlooks this part of the river.
name: Wide River

desc:
A wide river flows through here, swiftly passing northwards over a shallow part
of the riverbed. Small ditches run from the edges of the west side of the river
into a bleak looking field. The eastern bank shows a great deal more life along
the rivers edge. Far to the north a tower on a great mound looks down on this
part of the river.
name: Wide River

desc:
This section of the river is still quite fast as it rounds this gentle curve,
bringing the water from the south along the curve and into the west. The water
is slightly dark with silt, but still fairly clear. A stone and earth mound to
the north rises sharply from the river until it reaches the ruined tower that
overlooks this river. A barren field stretches along the waters edge to the
east.
name: Gentle Curve in the River

desc:
This small, fast river flows from the east, through here, into the north. A few
large rocks can be seen above the waterline, with more below causing a few
ripples and eddies at the waters surface. Despite the rocks there is ample room
for people to safely travel along the river.
name: Swift River

desc:
A slightly muddy river runs from the south of here, widening and slowing as it
flows into the north. A plain lies to the west, only slightly higher than the
normal waterline which is marked on shattered stone blocks. East is a high
artificial hill of grey stone blocks and earth upon which a shattered tower
overlooks this river.
name: Slightly Muddy Tributary River

desc:
Near the water the greenery springs into life, growing well above your head and
limiting sight to only a few yards. The sounds of small insects and of a swiftly
running river can be heard, somewhere beyond the green curtain that blocks sight
of the water.
name: Overlooking a River

desc:
This plain runs alongside the ancient road to the east. It extends towards the
north, covered by a variety of bushy plants. To the west, the ground slopes
upwards towards the top of a cliff or promontory. The grass underfoot is thin
and is shadowed by bushes, creating bare patches on the ground. To the east, the
road runs by north and south.
name: Bush Covered Plain

desc:
A flat, rock covered plain lies here covered in a green grass of average height
with a thin blade. A number of bushes have pushed their roots into the thin soil
and the cracks in the rock beneath. West and south are drops from cliffs, but
the one to the west is quite a longer drop, perhaps ninety feet, while the one
to the south is only ten feet or so to the river there.
name: Clifftop

desc:
A few large bushes spot this rock-covered plain with short green grass growing
around the bushes and between the rocks. Several trees grow near the western
edge where there is a sheer drop down the cliff. The plain continues into the
east. To the north and south lie other plains above the same cliff that lies
west of here.
name: Bush Covered Plain above a Cliff

desc:
This field is slightly narrow with the river pressing from the west and the
cliff from the east. Green grass and lichen cover this field on the soil and the
rocks respectively. A wall of jagged, tumbling rock prevents travel to the
south.
name: Narrow Field

desc:
This plain is covered in grass with little rock showing through except along the
edge of the cliff to the west. Along the west side of the plain at the cliffs
edge are many small cracks and, in places, missing pieces of rock. Several small
sections are unsupported and could slide down the face of the cliff with little
notice. The cracks become fewer towards the north.
name: Green Clifftop

desc:
A rocky field lies here, somewhat jagged because of the large areas where rock
has been thrust from the ground. Several types of grass cover this field, the
yellow and green grasses growing well in the deep soil between the stone areas,
and very poorly in the cracks of the stone outcroppings. Patches of lichen cover
much of the stone. Southwards rises a cliff, and to the east a large stone
outcrop makes travel impossible that way.
name: Rock Filled Field

desc:
Large and small rocks cover this field, with a few scattered patches of soil for
grass to grow in. In some places the rocks sit on top of each other forming
large piles that rise several feet above ground, and extend into the ground. The
uneven size of the rocks allow small tunnels to be formed in the piles, possibly
leading down into the soil beneath. A cliff with a clear view of this field can
be seen to the far north.
name: A Field of Scattered Rock

desc:
A small trail runs through this flat, grassy field. Newer growths of bushes and
other marks, such as ruts on the edges of the trail, show that the trail was at
one time more widely used. The trail has not been cut back in quite some time
and the vegetation has grown inwards leaving it only a few feet across. The
plant life is so low to the ground though that travel through it in any
direction is fairly easy except to the north where a small drop over jagged
rocks leads to a river. To the west a tower overlooks this area, as does a cliff
beyond the river to the north.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
This field is covered in rocks of the same type as the cliff that rises to the
east. Most of the rocks lie at the foot of the cliff that runs north and south
here. The cliff and the river along the western edge move toward each other to
the north and to the south.
name: Wide Rocky Field

desc:
Grass covers large amounts of this field, but not everywhere. The grass seems to
die off farther towards the northern border of the field. Bare earth shows where
the grass has died. A quickly moving, wide river flows along the western edge of
this field, and the land becomes more rocky but still covered with some grass
towards the east and south.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
Little of green can be seen in this barren field, except some small patches of
grass and a few small bushes. The shallow soil that covers this area is not
providing much of what the plants need to grow, so what little plant life that
exists here is small. A river runs to the west of here though the soil seems not
to have absorbed any moisture from it. Fields extend off to the east, south and
north and appear to be in a similar better condition to this field.
name: Barren Field

desc:
This large field has no trees in it and shows sign of having been burned several
times. A fairly large, but now somewhat overgrown, trail leads east to another
field, and west upwards to the ruined tower that overlooks this field. Bones and
small pieces of rusted metal can be seen on the ground all over the field, but
they are very dense within a bow shot of the tower. The river flows along the
northern edge of this field past a small spit sticking out into it.
name: Small Trail in a Field of Bones

desc:
The water in the river to the north has a light blue colour, indicating that it
is probably not as fast flowing as the river section northeast. It might be one
of the spots where it is easy to swim to the other side. A high cliff looms
above to the south, making travel in that direction impossible. However, a
skilled climber would be able to climb up to the summit by making use of various
outcroppings as handholds. This trail continues on, both to the east and west
here.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
The small trail carefully snakes its way through the plains. This section seems
to have once been part of the flood plain of the River Bruinen, lying below the
fields to the south, and sloping gently upwards to the east and west. Entry into
the river to the north would be fairly easy at this location.
name: Narrow Trail

desc:
Summertime flowers make a yellow and orange carpet bordering the large road to
the east. As the field moves away from the road, the vegetation becomes sparse
and lacks the greenery that one would expect to find in the vicinity of large
bodies of water. To the south the ground is bright green and covered with
clover.
name: Barren Field

desc:
This is the corner of a great field that continues northwest. Southeast is a
large and well-travelled road. Once in a while creatures and other living things
use this field as a shortcut while travelling north to south or vice versa. A
large green field is visible to the west, offering an area where one might take
a break from an arduous journey.
name: Grazing Area

desc:
This field extends on eastwards as a bright green blanket of healthy grass mixed
with clover during most of the year, only yielding in the heart of winter. In
contrast, the field to the north is almost completely bereft of greenery. The
ground slopes upwards towards the west, leading to a much higher outcropping. To
the south bushes seem to dominate the view and the road is barely visible in the
distance.
name: Grazing Area

desc:
Situated just west of the road, this section of plains is covered with a variety
of bushes and bushy plants. Signs of small burrowing animals and snakes can be
seen here and there on the ground, either tiny holes or collections of twigs and
leaves. The ground continues in this way towards the south and slopes upwards
towards the west.
name: Bush Covered Plain

desc:
During the summer months the sun turns this field light green and yellow,
breathing a small amount of life into it. When the nights start to become
shorter this area wakes up to begin its difficult existence. The short grass
starts to grow again and the few evident flowers struggle to bloom. To the east,
the same sort of barren terrain continues. Towards the north, a stone outcrop or
cliff rises quickly up out of the ground.
name: Barren Field

desc:
The ground angles sharply here, jutting up from the desolate fields that lie to
the east and south. There is not much vegetation here; the ground is almost
completely covered with stones and large rocks. However, a very few species of
grass are struggling to survive here and there. To the north one can see a trail
running below the ridge, and the River Bruinen coursing in the distance.
name: Top of a Cliff

desc:
A small section of floor remains here, no more than a few yards square. The
tower walls which once must have continued higher now range from floor level to
several feet high along the outside edge of this platform. The tower walls
farther around the circle do not even rise to this height. The height of the
tower gives this floor a commanding view of the rivers to the north, south and
west, and the trail to the east that is the only land route to this tower.
name: Shattered Top of the Tower

desc:
This tower is heavily damaged, almost destroyed. The circular tower wall of grey
stone is now more of a half circle with almost no eastern or southern sections.
Only a small piece of the floor of the level above remains and is now more of a
platform than a tower level. That small platform overlooks this level from above
and to the side, leaving this floor open to sunlight and weather. Some stairs
along the curved wall lead up to the remainder of the floor above, though not
many supports remain for the stairs or the upper floor. Some stairs also lead
underneath this level.
name: Ground Floor of a Ruined Tower

desc:
This large circular cellar is almost entirely made of stone. Only a doorway
sized area to the west is not made of stone, being filled instead with earth and
large stone blocks which have spilled out into this chamber. The support pillars
seem to be in good condition, but the rafters that help to support the levels
above have been heavily damaged by fire. A small stone staircase leads upwards
along the curve of the wall.
name: Cellar

desc:
The river flows fast here, with the water rushing loudly from the east into the
west. The water has many ripples and bits of foam in it. The soil in the plain
north of here is quite silty, due to runoff water from the river. A field
borders the river in the south.
name: River Bruinen

desc:
Water roars through this bend in the river. The water swiftly flows from the
north and is compressed by the southern cliff wall and the rocks that make up
the rapids. The churning water is then spit out to the west at a dangerous rate.
The water smashing against the cliff wall and the rocks make it easy for
travellers to hit one or the other.
name: Rapids on the River Bruinen

desc:
The trail seems to have been a path leading to some location eastwards, but all
that is visible in that direction is rolling fields of flowers, bordered farther
by some sort of scorched or blackened grassland. What remains of this trail
leads north and west towards the road. On the southern edge of the road thick
grass replaces the brightly coloured flowers that lie east.
name: Overgrown Trail

desc:
The thick, green grass grows tall along the hard-packed surface of the road to
the west. The northern edge seems bushier, and a trail leading in that direction
has been overgrown for what appears to be many years. The dense grass grows even
more abundantly towards the south, mixed with small white flowers that seem to
grow year-round. To the east the grass hosts quite a few bright flowers.
name: Thick Grass by a Road

desc:
The soft grass waves in the wind all year round except when the snow makes it
sleep under its white sheet. During the summer the grass is dense, the leaves
are light green, and the buds are yellow before it sets its seed. During the
winter it all turns a pale white colour, waiting for the spring. Towards the
east yellow and white are replaced by blue.
name: Grassland

desc:
The dense blanket of green grass is interrupted only by the dark brown surface
of the road to the west. The blanket of green seems to shift from dark to light
when wind blows across its surface. Towards the east, small yellow flowers dot
the landscape, and clumps of clover seem to huddle in between dense patches of
thick green grass.
name: Flowing Grass

desc:
The grass grows shorter near the road to the west, undoubtedly trampled down by
travellers or perhaps grazed by the various mounts that travel the road. Signs
of cows and a few other herbivores are visible here and there, as well as small
holes that might be signs of snake activity. Towards the east, yellow flowers
are visible.
name: Flowing Grass

desc:
Dense green grass dominates the view for miles around. The surface of the ground
is fairly even, making it easy to see for quite a distance in every direction.
Towards the east yellow flowers grow in abundance, leading on to some marshy
ground visible farther away. A road runs along the edge of a large bushy plain
in the west. This place would seem a good location to take a break from
travelling in relative safety.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Not much can be seen around here, except the endlessly flowing grass that covers
the plains totally. As a gust of wind ripples through the plants, it resembles
an ocean, with waves endlessly rolling across the vast expanse.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
As the river makes a few turns, a small section of plains has been almost
completely surrounded by water. The area does somewhat resemble an island,
completely covered by grass and a few small trees. Just on the other side of the
river, a dark and ominous tower rises from a wall of dense thorns.
name: Plains at the River

desc:
Closing in on the trail, the grass and plants try to reach out and cover the
remnants of civilisation. Not many marks of man can be seen here, but a careful
eye might notice small fragments that still recall the power of ages past.
name: Plains beside the Trail

desc:
Bordered on the west and north by a large road, the plains seem an ideal
location for taking a break on one's journey. This may have once been part of a
larger farmland, as there are raised bits of ground that could be where a farmer
would have placed posts for a fence or some other enclosure. In fact, on closer
inspection, one might notice that the raised bumps are square in shape. This
grassy area continues on towards a more open plain to the east.
name: Plains beside the Trail

desc:
Grassy flatlands stretch to the north and west while, to the south, the
vegetation thickens near the water. Splashing noises can be heard from the river
as it flows rapidly by and birds can be heard, hidden in the undergrowth. Just
east of you, a small hill rises above the plateau.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This hill separates two markedly different areas. To the west the grasslands
stretch for many miles, while to the east lie the swamps, an area of noisome
brown and blackened water. A river running south of here has eroded some of the
southern flank, making the slope down that direction rather steep. The view is
excellent, giving a good outlook over the surrounding areas. Far to the
southwest the remains of an old castle can be seen, with one tower still
standing.
name: A Hill by the River

desc:
Below the water surface, a strip of hard and firm ground runs in a straight line
from the north to the south. Here used to pass an old trail, but it has now
sunken below, as the swamps reclaimed their land. Still, it will be of use to
you, as it provides sure footing in this marshy, water-covered place. Even so,
the support might prove short and treacherous as there is no way to tell if the
trail ends.
name: A Sunken Trail

desc:
A small line of hills rises out of the marshland. Between the hills flow several
small streams. Over the hills runs a trail, stretching out from north to south.
Almost completely overgrown, it still provides sure footing for you and has kept
many a weary traveller from wandering out into the swamps and the mud.
name: An Old Path through the Hills

desc:
The ground here is muddy with quite a bit of groundwater. The marsh must be
formed by a combination of the run-off water from the grounds to the north and
the underground water source that seems to seep into the soil from deep under
the ground. Towards the south a small line of hills is visible, with a path
leading around them.
name: Marshy Plains

desc:
The land here does not have the same fate as the small pasture lying to the
west, where the bright green grass grows abundantly. Here, on lower ground, the
grass grows only on the slopes leading west and south. Year after year, a bit
more solid ground is eroded away by the torrents of rain water flowing from the
west, and the ever-rising waters of the marshy plains to the south.
name: Open-ditch Drainage

desc:
This low-lying area serves almost as a pit for the surrounding plains. Runoff
water and vegetation that is swept by it flow into this secluded area. Surface
rock deep at the bottom of this quagmire prevents the water from being absorbed
into the ground. The situation in the south does not help, as the small slope
running from that direction encourages more water to flow into the quagmire.
name: Quagmire

desc:
Running just to the east of a ridge this wet ground is covered with a soft moss
instead of the typical grass that one might find in these surroundings. East of
here grass grows abundantly, although the ground is still quite soft. To the
south the situation becomes even more severe; the field there seems to have
collapsed or sunken into the ground across a one mile radius.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
Lying at the bottom of a ridge line, and on lower ground than the surrounding
area, this gully is quite muddy. Any rain or snowmelt that occurs within a few
miles is either absorbed by the ground where it falls, or runs down here, which
keeps the ground muddy. It would be impossible to ride here, and it is a bit
difficult for even a mountain goat to keep its footing.
name: Muddy Ground

desc:
Clumps of green and yellow grass grow sparsely throughout the blackened remains
of what was once a fertile field. Swathes of blackened trees and grass mark what
must have been a wildfire sweeping across the plains. Towards the south the
source of the blaze seems evident. The ground is dry and cracked, and does not
support any life.
name: Burnt Field

desc:
During spring and early summer an abundance of colourful flowers bloom into life
to adorn the normally solid green landscape with bright yellow, orange and blue
splashes of colour. Towards the east the ground has more of a scorched
appearance, and even the grass struggles to grow along the edges. Towards the
west a trail is visible, but is densely overgrown by thorny shrubs.
name: Mixed Flower Field

desc:
Despite the poor soil that is evident in the surroundings closely cropped grass
and a few flowers manage to grow. Towards the west the grass grows much more
lushly for some distance as it approaches the brown earth of the road. Off in
the distance southeast some sort of ruins are visible, just south of a large
blackened section of ground.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
The ground is dry and cracked like a pond when it dries up during the hot
summer. However, no amount of rain seems to improve it. This tiny plateau sits
on higher ground, so any water that might fall here immediately flows into the
surrounding lower ground. To the east, one could probably climb down to the wet
ground below.
name: Scorched Earth

desc:
A few low stones in a roughly square shape are all that remain of what was once
some sort of dwelling. Perhaps a hermit or someone once lived here, but the
hovel was long ago abandoned. A few small broken tools lie on the ground here
and there, and what looks like the remains of a trapdoor are in the centre of
the dwelling, covered by a large rock. The paths to east and south are both
blocked by large hedges.
name: Insignificant Ruin

desc:
No matter what time of year one might travel through this area, the entire
landscape looks as if it is on the verge of blooming to life. In the warm
seasons of the year small blooms can be seen throughout the grassy terrain. To
the east a copse of bushes grows on the edge of some wasteland and there is some
sort of ruins visible beyond them.
name: A Fertile Field

desc:
The soil underfoot is quite dry, seemingly inhospitable, yet bright green grass
and a variety of yellow flowers grow in patches. Towards the south the same
yellow flowers dominate the landscape. The ground leads down gently to the east,
